policy_statement: Support the transition of taxi, for-hire, and limousine vehicles to low-carbon fuels and technologies.
mechanism_description: The city supports shifting taxi, for-hire, and limousine vehicles to lower-carbon fuels and technologies, reducing transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transportation service (vehicle fleet operation) → fuel/technology switch → ↓ fuel combustion/lifecycle carbon intensity → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Operation and procurement/turnover of taxi, for-hire, and limousine fleets, shifting from gasoline/diesel vehicles and fuels to lower-carbon technologies/fuels (battery-electric, hydrogen, renewable diesel, biofuels, etc.).  
2) Mediating physical process: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and often CH₄/N₂O) from reduced/avoided fossil fuel combustion and/or lower upstream (well-to-wheel) carbon intensity of the energy carrier; potentially improved drivetrain efficiency (e.g., EVs) further reduces energy use per mile.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂e) attributable to the targeted fleets.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from taxi/for-hire/limousine operations by switching to lower-carbon fuels/technologies.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Vehicle fleet fuel/technology transition lowers the carbon intensity of mobility service, yielding direct GHG reductions from on-road transportation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although EVs can also improve energy efficiency, the defining intent and largest climate effect of the mechanism is emissions mitigation (decarbonizing the energy source), not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not nature-based carbon sequestration, and not purely resource-efficiency independent of emissions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “low-carbon fuels” include options with contested lifecycle benefits (some biofuels) or if grid electricity is high-carbon, the magnitude may vary, but the intended dominant pathway remains emissions reduction via lower-carbon energy.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that supports/mandates shifting taxis/for-hire/limos to low-carbon fuels/technologies shares the same primary causal mechanism of reducing transport GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtype details (e.g., EV-only vs renewable diesel vs hydrogen; tailpipe-only vs lifecycle accounting; equity/air-quality co-benefits) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle emissions depend on fuel pathway and electricity grid mix; some “low-carbon” fuels may deliver limited or uncertain GHG reductions, but the mechanism’s dominant intent/effect remains mitigation through transport decarbonization.
key_indicators: “Support the transition”; “taxi, for-hire, and limousine vehicles”; “low-carbon fuels & technologies”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/PM), reduced noise, potential fuel/maintenance cost savings for drivers/fleet operators
instance_edge_case_notes: Text does not specify the support mechanism (rebates, financing, infrastructure, regulatory changes). Classified as an incentive-program/enabling based on the generic “support” phrasing; could alternatively be infrastructure-investment or planning-strategy if the underlying implementation is non-financial.
